title: Quiz: Model Evaluation and Validation
description: Test your understanding of evaluating quantum credit scoring models

questions.question: Why is AUC often preferred over accuracy for credit scoring evaluation?

options.text: AUC is always higher than accuracy
AUC measures discrimination across all thresholds, not just one
Accuracy is not a valid metric

questions.explanation: AUC (Area Under ROC Curve) evaluates model performance across all possible classification thresholds, making it more informative than accuracy which depends on a single threshold. This is crucial when class imbalance exists, as in credit scoring.

questions.question: What does a KS statistic of 0.35 indicate for a credit scoring model?

options.text: Good separation between good and bad borrowers

questions.explanation: A KS statistic of 0.35 indicates good separation between defaulters and non-defaulters. KS values above 0.30 are generally considered acceptable for credit models, with higher values indicating better discrimination ability.

questions.question: What is demographic parity in the context of credit model fairness?

options.text: Equal positive prediction rates across protected groups
Equal number of samples from each group

questions.explanation: Demographic parity requires that the model predicts positive outcomes (e.g., loan approval) at equal rates across protected groups. It focuses on the outcome distribution regardless of true labels.

questions.question: What is the regulatory significance of a disparate impact ratio below 0.8?

options.text: The model may face legal challenges for discrimination
The model needs more training data

questions.explanation: The 4/5ths (80%) rule is a common regulatory threshold. If one group's approval rate is less than 80% of another's, this indicates potential disparate impact and may require further investigation or model adjustment.

questions.question: Stratified cross-validation is important for credit scoring because it maintains the class distribution (default rate) in each fold.
questions.explanation: True. Credit scoring data is typically imbalanced (low default rate). Stratified CV ensures each fold has the same proportion of defaults, providing more reliable performance estimates than random splits.

questions.question: What does the Brier score measure for a credit scoring model?

options.text: How well the model discriminates between classes
How well-calibrated the predicted probabilities are

questions.explanation: The Brier score measures calibration - how close predicted probabilities are to actual outcomes. A lower Brier score indicates better calibration, meaning predicted probabilities accurately reflect true default rates.

questions.question: When comparing quantum and classical credit models, which comparison is most fair?

options.text: Using different datasets for each model
Using the same train/test split and evaluation metrics
Giving quantum models more training data
Optimizing hyperparameters only for classical models

questions.explanation: Fair comparison requires using identical train/test splits, evaluation metrics, and giving both models similar hyperparameter tuning opportunities. This ensures any performance differences are due to the model type, not experimental setup.

questions.question: Which are important considerations for credit model validation? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Credit model validation must include discrimination metrics (how well it separates good/bad), fairness assessment (no illegal discrimination), and calibration (accurate probabilities). These are all regulatory and business requirements.
